verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: These suggestions are not made to you, of course, to be developed into an entire crudity and wildness of appearance; nor are we seeking to persuade you of the good of squalor and slovenliness; but of the limit and norm and just measure of cultivation of the person.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: There must be no overstepping of that line to which simple and sufficient refinements limit their desires—that line which is pleasing to God.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: For they who rub their skin with medicaments, stain their cheeks with rouge, make their eyes prominent with antimony, sin against Him.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: To them, I suppose, the plastic skill of God is displeasing!

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: In their own persons, I suppose, they convict, they censure, the Artificer of all things!

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: For censure they do when they amend, when they add to, (His work;) taking these their additions, of course, from the adversary artificer.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: That adversary artificer is the devil.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: For who would show the way to change the body, but he who by wickedness transfigured man’s spirit?

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: He it is, undoubtedly, who adapted ingenious devices of this kind; that in your persons it may be apparent that you, in a certain sense, do violence to God.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: Whatever is born is the work of God.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: Whatever, then, is plastered on (that), is the devil’s work.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: To superinduce on a divine work Satan’s ingenuities, how criminal is it!

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: Our servants borrow nothing from our personal enemies: soldiers eagerly desire nothing from the foes of their own general; for, to demand for (your own) use anything from the adversary of Him in whose hand you are, is a transgression.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: Shall a Christian be assisted in anything by that evil one?

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: (If he do,) I know not whether this name (of “Christian”) will continue (to belong) to him; for he will be his in whose lore he eagerly desires to be instructed.

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: But how alien from your schoolings and professions are (these things)!

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: How unworthy the Christian name, to wear a fictitious face, (you,) on whom simplicity in every form is enjoined!—to lie in your appearance, (you,) to whom (lying) with the tongue is not lawful!—to seek after what is another’s, (you,) to whom is delivered (the precept of) abstinence from what is another’s!—to practise adultery in your mien, (you,) who make modesty your study!

verses.book: On the Apparel of Women - Book II
verses.text: Think, blessed (sisters), how will you keep God’s precepts if you shall not keep in your own persons His lineaments?
